title: How Uber Builds Zone-Failure-Resilient OpenSearch Clusters
gist: Uber describes how it fixed chronic yellow-state, unassigned-shard, and disk-skew problems in multi-zone OpenSearch clusters by inserting a logical isolation-group abstraction between physical zones and OpenSearch's placement logic, plus a 5-manager quorum design, achieving 100% shard assignment and green health across all Tier 3+ clusters without forking OpenSearch.

themes.theme: Isolation groups as a resilience abstraction

insights.claim: Uber fixed OpenSearch allocation problems caused by physically unbalanced zone node counts by introducing isolation groups (IGs) -- a logical layer guaranteeing an equal number of nodes per group regardless of underlying physical zone imbalance, with node IG membership persisting through hardware replacements.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: A reusable architectural pattern for any team running multi-zone OpenSearch/Elasticsearch where physical zone capacity is uneven and causes disk skew or unassigned shards.

insights.claim: Most Uber services, including OpenSearch, run 3 isolation groups mapped one-to-one with physical zones, so a single zone failure removes at most about a third of capacity, and each index runs with a minimum of 2 replicas (3 total shard copies), one per IG, giving baseline resilience against loss of a single group.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Concrete capacity-planning benchmark (3 IGs, 3 shard copies) for teams designing similar zone-resilient search deployments.
insights.locator: Most Uber services, including OpenSearch, run 3 IGs

insights.claim: By declaring all expected isolation-group attribute values upfront (forced shard allocation awareness), OpenSearch recognizes a missing IG during a zone outage and refuses to over-allocate shards to the remaining groups, so affected shards stay unassigned and the cluster turns yellow instead of triggering a disruptive rebalancing storm across healthy nodes.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Solves a well-known OpenSearch failure mode (rebalancing storms overloading disk/CPU/network during zone loss) with a configuration-only technique, not a fork or custom engine.
insights.locator: The harder problem is what happens during the failure itself

insights.claim: Uber uses 5 cluster manager nodes instead of the typical 3, combined with OpenSearch's auto_shrink_voting_configuration setting, so a zone failure taking out up to 2 managers still leaves a writable 3-node cluster with 2-of-3 quorum, and even a subsequent single additional node failure still leaves 2 nodes and retains quorum -- a sequence a standard 3-manager cluster cannot survive.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: A concrete, adoptable configuration (5 managers + auto-shrink voting) for surviving a zone-plus-node failure sequence without a custom fork.
insights.locator: Uber uses 5 cluster manager nodes for quorum

insights.claim: After deploying the isolation-group abstraction across all Tier 3 and higher OpenSearch/Elasticsearch clusters, Uber reports 100% shard assignment and consistently green cluster health, eliminating the previous yellow-state and shard-assignment failures caused by zone-size asymmetry.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Quantified proof point that the pattern works at Uber's scale and is applicable broadly, since it builds on standard OpenSearch/Elasticsearch features rather than requiring a custom search engine.
insights.locator: Uber reports that the IG abstraction fixed yellow-state

insights.claim: The same isolation-group model is applied at Uber to Apache Pinot, mapping isolation-group IDs to replica-group pools so that segment replicas survive a complete zone failure, reflecting a wider industry trend of embedding failure-domain awareness directly in the data layer rather than relying solely on infrastructure-level failover.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Signals a broader design pattern -- data-layer failure-domain awareness -- worth tracking across other high-scale data infrastructure vendors and adopters.
insights.locator: Uber's method reflects a wider trend in the industry
